pages.title: Teen Therapy in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Child Therapy  /  Teen Therapy in Louisville, KY

Teen therapy in Louisville, KY, gives teens a place to talk. It also helps parents make sense of changes at home. A family may reach out when a teen seems sad, worried, angry, shut down, or hard to reach. This kind of adolescent counseling can help the family slow down and learn what the teen needs.

Call (502) 416-1416

At Louisville Mental Health Group, teen counseling starts with what is happening now. We listen to the teen and the parent. Then we help the family find a plan that fits. The plan may change as needs become clear.

When This May Help

Teen therapy may help when a teen has:

  • Mood changes, sadness, or anger
  • Worry, panic, stress, or fear
  • School stress or trouble keeping up
  • Social stress or friendship problems
  • More time alone or less interest in things
  • Family conflict or trouble talking at home
  • Grief, trauma, or a major life change

Parents may see a change but not know why it began. Teens may also know that something feels wrong but have a hard time saying it. You do not need to know the cause before you reach out.

Some families are not sure where to start. Child Therapy is often for kids ages 2 to 12. Teen therapy is made for the needs of teens and their families.

What Support Can Look Like

Teen therapy often includes time for the teen to meet with the therapist alone. Parents or caregivers may also join check-ins or family sessions. At the first visit, the therapist explains what can stay private. The therapist also explains what may be shared and when a safety concern or law requires sharing. When possible, the therapist talks with the teen before sharing and shares only what is needed.

LMHG may use a team approach when it helps. A teen may have one main therapist. Another therapist may join if a special skill is needed. Each role should stay clear. For some older teens, Individual Counseling may be a better fit.

Teen Therapy, Child Therapy, And Family Work

Teen therapy gives teens more say in the work. It also gives them more room to talk alone. In child therapy, parents are often more involved. This is more common with young children.

Parents can still be part of teen therapy. They may share what has changed. They may help with home routines. They may also join a session when it makes sense.

At times, the main issue affects the whole home. In that case, Family Therapy may be added. It may also be the best place to start. The family and therapist can decide this together.

FAQs

pages.title: Adult Counseling in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Individual Counseling  /  Adult Counseling in Louisville, KY

Adult counseling in Louisville, KY, gives you a private place to talk. You can start with what feels hard now. This may be stress, worry, low mood, grief, anger, trauma, or a big change. Adult therapy at LMHG is shaped around the person. There is no one plan for each client.

Call (502) 416-1416

When This May Help

Adult counseling may help when:

  • Stress keeps building
  • Worry is hard to turn off
  • Low mood gets in the way of daily life
  • Grief or past events still feel heavy
  • Work stress takes up too much space
  • Anger or conflict keeps coming back
  • A relationship feels strained
  • A life change leaves you feeling stuck

You do not need to know the right therapy term. You can begin by saying what is going on. You can also share what you want to change or understand.

What Support Can Look Like

Adult counseling is one-on-one therapy. Early talks help the therapist learn what brings you in. You can set goals together. You can also decide what pace feels useful.

Sessions may look at thoughts, feelings, habits, and stress. They may include skills you can use between visits. Some talks may focus on grief or trauma. Others may focus on work, family, or hard choices.

The method should fit the reason you came to therapy. You may meet with one main therapist on a regular basis. If a more focused service may help, another therapist can join the team. For example, a client may also work with a provider who offers Trauma Therapy. Each therapist should have a clear role.

LMHG accepts Medicaid, commercial insurance, and private pay options when the plan fits. For more details, see Costs and Insurance.

Adult Counseling And Individual Counseling

Adult counseling is a form of Individual Counseling. It has a clear focus on adult needs. These may include work stress, parenting stress, grief, burnout, or long-term patterns.

Adult counseling is not the same as Couples Counseling. It is also not family therapy. In adult counseling, one adult has a therapist and a private place to talk. A couple or family service may be added when it would help. A different therapist may lead that work. This helps keep roles and limits clear.

faqItems.question: Is adult counseling private?
faqItems.answer: Yes. It is one-on-one therapy with privacy limits. Your therapist can explain those limits at the start.

faqItems.question: What can I talk about in adult therapy?
faqItems.answer: You can talk about stress, worry, low mood, grief, anger, trauma, work, relationships, or life changes.

faqItems.question: Can I work with more than one LMHG therapist?
faqItems.answer: Sometimes. You may have a main therapist and another therapist for a more focused need. Their roles should stay clear.

faqItems.question: Do I need to choose a therapy method first?
faqItems.answer: No. Your therapist can help choose methods based on your needs and the reason you reached out.

pages.title: Anxiety and Depression Therapy in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Individual Counseling  /  Anxiety and Depression Therapy in Louisville, KY

Anxiety and depression therapy gives you a place to talk. It can help with worry, low mood, stress, and daily strain. LMHG offers therapy and counseling. It does not manage medicine.

Call (502) 416-1416

When This May Help

Anxiety and depression can look different from one person to the next. You may want help when you notice:

  • Thoughts that race or will not slow down
  • Worry, fear, or panic
  • Low mood or a numb feeling
  • Low drive or less interest in things
  • Sleep changes or feeling worn down
  • Avoiding people, places, school, or work
  • Stress that hurts daily routines
  • Grief, family stress, or a major change

You do not need to know the cause before you reach out. You also do not need a diagnosis. You can start with what you notice. Then you and a therapist can talk about what may fit.

What Support Can Look Like

Therapy may help you spot patterns and triggers. It may also help you build coping skills. You and the therapist can make a plan for daily life. The plan should fit your needs and goals.

Sessions may look at thoughts, feelings, habits, and stress. They may also look at sleep, school, work, or home life. The pace should make sense for you. You do not have to tell every hard detail at once.

Anxiety therapy and depression counseling are often part of Individual Counseling. Some clients work with one therapist. Others may use LMHG's team approach. If past events are a main concern, Trauma Therapy may also fit.

Therapy, Psychiatry, And Medication Care

Therapy and medication care have different roles. LMHG provides therapy and counseling. We do not prescribe or manage medicine. We also do not replace a medical provider.

Some people use therapy and medication care at the same time. The services come from separate providers. A therapist can work on coping skills, patterns, goals, and daily plans. A medical or psychiatry provider should answer questions about medicine.

This page is about LMHG therapy. It does not give medical advice. It also does not promise that symptoms will change.

faqItems.question: Does LMHG provide psychiatry or medication management?
faqItems.answer: No. LMHG provides anxiety therapy and depression counseling. We do not prescribe or manage medicine.

faqItems.question: Can therapy be used with medication care?
faqItems.answer: Yes. Some clients use both through separate providers. Medicine questions should go to a medical or psychiatry provider.

faqItems.question: Do I need a diagnosis before reaching out?
faqItems.answer: No. You can start with what you notice, such as worry, low mood, panic, stress, or feeling stuck.

faqItems.question: Does therapy guarantee that symptoms will improve?
faqItems.answer: No. Therapy may help you learn about patterns and build skills. No change or result can be promised.

pages.title: Parent-Child Attachment Therapy in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Family Therapy  /  Parent-Child Attachment Therapy in Louisville, KY

Parent-child attachment therapy in Louisville, KY, helps a child and parent rebuild trust. It may fit after stress, time apart, loss, moves, or other hard change. This attachment therapy for children looks at the bond between a child and parent. It may also use family therapy for attachment concerns when more family members need to take part.

Call (502) 416-1416

This work often fits within family therapy. It may also connect with child therapy when the child needs their own support.

When This May Help

You may be looking for help because:

  • A child pulls away or shuts down with a parent.
  • A child seems guarded or does not ask for comfort.
  • A parent is not sure how to respond to big feelings.
  • Trust has been hurt by stress, loss, or time apart.
  • A move or other family change has been hard.
  • Daily routines lead to fear or power struggles.

These signs do not tell us the cause on their own. A therapist can help the family look at the full picture and choose a clear plan.

What Support Can Look Like

Sessions may include the child, the parent, the family, or a mix. The plan will depend on the child's age, family history, safety needs, and current stress.

The therapist may help the parent spot patterns and slow down hard moments. Parents can learn ways to stay calm, set clear roles, and respond in ways the child can grasp. The child may get help naming feelings and making up after conflict.

The work may cover:

  • Calm home routines.
  • Repair after an argument.
  • Clear roles for adults and children.
  • Simple ways to talk about hard topics.
  • Parent coaching and check-ins.

Some families need help with one short-term strain. Others need a slower plan. LMHG does not assume one provider can meet every need. Another provider may help when a child or parent needs a different type of therapy.

How This Fits With Family And Child Therapy

Family therapy is broad. It may focus on conflict, routines, stress, or how family members talk with each other. Attachment therapy has a tighter focus on trust between a child and parent.

Child therapy gives a child their own place to work on feelings, behavior, stress, or trauma. When the main concern is the parent-child bond, the parent will often take part too.

LMHG must review court-related family needs before naming the right service. Attachment therapy is not a custody review, legal opinion, or promise that a family will reunite.

faqItems.question: Is this page about adult attachment styles?
faqItems.answer: No. At LMHG, this service is only for trust and repair between a child and parent. LMHG is not adding a separate adult attachment page at this time.

faqItems.question: Does a parent take part?
faqItems.answer: Often, yes. Parent involvement matters when the main concern is the bond between the child and parent.

faqItems.question: Can this help after a family change?
faqItems.answer: It may fit when loss, time apart, a move, or another change has hurt trust. LMHG will review the details first.

faqItems.question: Is this the same as family therapy?
faqItems.answer: It may be part of family therapy, but it has a tighter focus on parent-child trust and repair.

pages.content:
Home  /  Mental Health Careers in Louisville, KY

Mental health careers in Louisville at LMHG for therapists, counselors, case managers, community support professionals, and care team roles that support accessible local care.

Work that connects clinical care with real Louisville needs.

Louisville Mental Health Group serves clients who may need office-based therapy, telehealth, child and family support, case management, community support, or coordinated care across home, school, and community settings. Careers here can involve direct clinical work, practical care coordination, documentation, scheduling support, and careful communication with clients and families.

If your background overlaps with individual counseling, child and teen therapy, family therapy, case management, community support services, or community-based mental health services, send a resume and a short note about the role you are interested in, your credentials or experience, and why the practice feels like a fit.

LMHG is especially interested in people who understand that Louisville mental health work is both clinical and practical: clients may need a provider who can listen well, explain options clearly, respect insurance and access barriers, and help families move from first contact to the right next step.

Career Fit Questions

What mental health roles can I ask LMHG about?

You can ask about therapist, counselor, case management, community support, administrative, and care-team roles. Current hiring needs may vary, so the best first step is to share your background and the role you are seeking.

What should I include when I reach out about a career?

Include a resume, your Kentucky credentials or relevant experience, the type of role you want, preferred setting, availability, and a short explanation of why LMHG's Louisville service mix fits your work.

Are LMHG roles only office-based?

Not necessarily. Depending on the role, service fit, and current needs, work may connect with office-based care, telehealth, home, school, or community settings. Ask about the setting expectations for the specific role.

Can newer clinicians or support professionals contact LMHG?

Yes. If your training, supervision needs, license status, or experience may fit LMHG's work, explain that clearly in your message so the office can respond based on current needs and requirements.

pages.title: Case Management in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Community-Based Services  /  Case Management in Louisville, KY

Case Management helps Medicaid clients with needs that can affect mental health. A case manager can help you make a plan. They can help you find aid. They can also help each person on your team work toward the same goals. This service is also called targeted case management.

Call (502) 416-1416

When Case Management May Help

It is hard to focus on your health when basic needs are not met. It is also hard when many problems happen at once. Case Management may help with:

  • finding help for basic needs;
  • dealing with housing stress;
  • building job skills;
  • keeping up with more than one provider;
  • working with a school on a plan;
  • making a crisis plan before a hard time becomes an emergency; and
  • learning to ask questions and speak up for your needs.

A case manager cannot promise a home, money, a ride, or other aid. The goal is to help you know your choices. Your case manager can then help you take the next step.

What Case Management Can Look Like

Your plan should fit your needs. A case manager may help you choose what comes first. They may make calls, send forms, plan for a meeting, or track next steps. With your OK, they may work with a therapist, doctor, school, health plan, or local group.

Case Management is not therapy. You may still see a therapist. Your case manager can help with the day-to-day parts of your plan. LMHG can link this work with community-based mental health services when needed.

Case Management And Community Support Are Different

Case Management focuses on plans, aid, team work, and speaking up for your needs. Community Support Services help a client use therapy goals in daily life.

Community Support Associates work like therapy aides. They work with the client's therapist. They may help the client use new skills at home, at school, or out in town. The client still sees the therapist often.

Some clients use one service. Others use both when each one has a clear role.

Paying For Case Management

Case Management is most often for Medicaid clients. Private pay may be an option in some cases. We must check the plan and service fit before work starts. Visit Costs and Insurance to learn more.

faqItems.question: Is Case Management the same as therapy?
faqItems.answer: No. Therapy focuses on thoughts, feelings, behavior, and therapy goals. Case Management focuses on daily needs, resources, plans, helping providers work together, and follow-through. A client may use both services.

faqItems.question: Does Case Management include crisis help?
faqItems.answer: A case manager can help make a crisis plan. Case Management is not an emergency service. It is not open 24 hours a day and does not replace emergency help.

faqItems.question: Can a case manager guarantee housing or other resources?
faqItems.answer: No. A case manager may help find options, complete steps, and speak with other groups. LMHG cannot promise housing, money, transportation, or any other resource.

faqItems.question: Does Medicaid cover Case Management?
faqItems.answer: Case Management is mostly paid for by Medicaid. Coverage depends on the plan and the client's needs. A private-pay plan with an agreed fee may be possible in some cases.

pages.title: Child Behavioral Therapy in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Child Therapy  /  Child Behavioral Therapy in Louisville, KY

Child behavioral therapy in Louisville, KY, helps parents make sense of behavior they see at home or school. The cause may not be clear yet. A child behavioral therapist looks at what happens before, during, and after the behavior. This child behavior therapy process helps the family build a plan that fits the child.

Call (502) 416-1416

This service is often used with children ages 2 to 12. It is one type of child therapy, not a one-size plan for every child.

When This May Help

This service may help when a child has:

  • Big reactions that are hard to calm.
  • Hitting, yelling, running off, or frequent refusal.
  • School calls or trouble with class rules.
  • Hard mornings, bedtimes, or screen limits.
  • Meltdowns or shutdowns after a change.
  • Stress after grief, trauma, a move, or family conflict.
  • Behavior that leaves parents unsure what to try next.

Parents do not need to know why the behavior is happening before they reach out. Starting with what you see is enough.

What Support Can Look Like

The plan may include time with the child, parent check-ins, family sessions, or help with home routines. School contact may also be part of the plan when it is useful and allowed.

The child may learn to name feelings, calm their body, and recover after conflict. Parents may learn how to use clear limits and steady follow-through. Younger children often need more parent help. Older children may spend more time alone with the therapist.

LMHG does not use the same plan for every child. Some children need more play-based work. Some need more work on routines and skills. Family therapy may help when stress involves more than one person at home.

This service does not include psychiatry or medicine. LMHG does not provide autism or learning tests. We also do not provide ABA, speech therapy, or occupational therapy. This service does not include custody opinions or crisis response.

How This Differs From Child Therapy And Play Therapy

Child therapy is the broad parent service. It can help with feelings, grief, trauma, school stress, behavior, or family change.

Child behavioral therapy has a closer focus on behavior that affects daily life. It looks at patterns, triggers, routines, skills, and parent response.

Play therapy uses play and other tools that fit the child's age. It can help a child show feelings that are hard to put into words. Child behavioral work may use play too, but its main focus stays on behavior patterns and daily skills.

faqItems.question: Does this service include medicine?
faqItems.answer: No. LMHG does not provide psychiatry or medicine management.

faqItems.question: Does LMHG provide autism or learning tests?
faqItems.answer: No. LMHG does not provide autism tests, learning tests, ABA, speech therapy, or occupational therapy.

faqItems.question: Will the therapist give a custody opinion?
faqItems.answer: No. This service does not include custody opinions, custody reviews, or legal advice.

faqItems.question: Is this crisis support?
faqItems.answer: No. This is not a crisis response service. Use emergency or crisis resources when there is an immediate safety concern.

pages.title: Child Therapy in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Child Therapy

Child therapy in Louisville, KY, helps parents make sense of what they see. It can also help them find a next step. At LMHG, child counseling is often for kids ages 2 to 12. You may see a change in mood or behavior but not know the cause. That is a fine place to start.

Call (502) 416-1416

When This May Help

You may want to ask about child counseling when your child has:

  • Big feelings or fast mood changes
  • Anger, worry, fear, or sadness
  • New behavior at home or school
  • Trouble with routines or rules
  • Stress after grief, trauma, or a family change
  • A hard time with friends, siblings, or adults
  • School stress or a drop in school work

These signs do not tell you the full cause. A therapist can learn more about your child, your family, and what is happening now.

What Support Can Look Like

There is no single plan for every child. The therapist picks an approach based on the child's age, needs, and main concern. Sessions may use play, talk, or skill practice.

Parent help is also shaped case by case. It may include parent check-ins, a family session, help with home routines, or talks with a school when needed and approved. Younger children often need a parent to take a larger role.

Parents may also need help using the plan between visits. This can include a simple routine, a new way to respond to behavior, or a way to help the child name a feeling before it grows.

The therapist will explain how parents will be involved. Parent check-ins and updates depend on the child's age, needs, and plan. The goal is to support the child while giving parents useful ways to help at home.

Child Therapy, Play Therapy, And Behavior Therapy

Child therapy is the broad service. Play Therapy and Child Behavioral Therapy are types of child therapy. The best fit depends on what your child shows, how they learn, and why you reached out.

Some children may also have a few Family Therapy sessions. An older child may fit better with Teen Therapy. LMHG can help you sort through these choices. You do not need to pick a method on your own.

faqItems.question: What ages does child therapy serve?
faqItems.answer: Child therapy at LMHG is generally for children ages 2 to 12. LMHG can help guide older children to a better fit.

faqItems.question: Will I be part of my child's therapy?
faqItems.answer: Parent help is based on the child and the goal. Younger children often need more parent involvement.

faqItems.question: Do I need to choose play or behavior therapy?
faqItems.answer: No. The therapist can help choose an approach after learning about your child and the main concern.

faqItems.question: Can the therapist work with my child's school?
faqItems.answer: School talks may be part of the plan when they are useful, approved, and allowed by the service.

pages.title: Co-Parenting Services in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Court Ordered Services  /  Co-Parenting Services in Louisville, KY

At LMHG, Co-Parenting Services help parents talk and make choices for their child. Help may start before conflict grows. It may also be court ordered. One parent or both may reach out.

Call (502) 416-1416

Each parent may meet alone. Both parents may also meet together. This is not a parenting class, mediation, or a custody evaluation.

When This May Help

Co-Parenting Services may help when:

  • talks often turn into blame, anger, or silence.
  • schedules and changes lead to repeat conflict.
  • a child feels caught in the middle.
  • parents do not agree on school or medical choices.
  • new partners add stress.
  • a blended family needs clearer roles.
  • court rules affect how parents work together.
  • parents want a plan before a small issue grows.

The main focus is the child. Parents do not have to agree on every point. The work can set clear ways to share facts and make plans. It keeps the child out of adult conflict.

What Support Can Look Like

Sessions may be one-on-one or joint. The provider will choose the format. The need and each person's role will guide that choice.

LMHG can review a court order before the work starts. Sessions may cover rules for contact. Parents may learn ways to share key facts. They may work on school plans or medical choices. Schedules and family changes may also be part of the work.

The provider stays impartial. They do not take sides. LMHG cannot promise less conflict or a set court result.

The plan can change as new concerns come up. The provider will keep the work tied to the child's needs and the goals set for the service.

How Co-Parenting Services Are Different

Some people search for co-parenting counseling in Louisville, KY. LMHG uses Co-Parenting Services as the public name. This work may start before a court issue. It may also be court ordered. It is not a parenting class. It does not give a class certificate.

Parenting Support looks at a parent's work with a child at home. Co-Parenting Services look at how adults parent across homes. Family Reunification Services have a different goal. They focus on parent-child contact after an attachment break. See Court-Ordered Services for the wider set of court-related options.

This service is not mediation or a custody evaluation. LMHG does not give legal advice. It does not offer supervised visitation or emergency response through this service. The provider does not take sides. They do not promise a court result.

faqItems.question: Can one parent start the process?
faqItems.answer: Yes. One parent can reach out. LMHG will review the need. The team will explain who may take part in the work.

faqItems.question: Can Co-Parenting Services be court ordered?
faqItems.answer: Yes. LMHG can provide court-ordered co-parenting intervention. The team can review a court order when one is present.

faqItems.question: Is this a parenting class or certificate program?
faqItems.answer: No. This is not a parenting class. LMHG does not give a class certificate for this service.

faqItems.question: Is this mediation or a custody evaluation?
faqItems.answer: No. LMHG does not settle legal disputes. It does not give custody opinions or decide which parent is right.

pages.title: Community-Based Mental Health Services in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Community-Based Mental Health Services in Louisville, KY

Community-based mental health services in Louisville, KY, can help when daily needs make therapy harder. LMHG can help clients plan next steps and connect with resources. The work can link you to help, teach skills, and help you act on a plan. It is often short and has a clear goal.

Call (502) 416-1416

This page explains how case management in Louisville, KY, and community support services in Louisville, KY, fit with therapy. Each service has its own role. A person may need one service or a small team.

LMHG is a small local practice. We can offer practical help that is often found only at a large agency. We look at the whole person, not just what happens in a therapy visit.

When This May Help

This kind of help may fit when daily needs make it hard to focus in therapy. It may include:

  • helping each provider use the same plan;
  • finding help for food, housing, or other basic needs;
  • learning to ask questions and speak up for your needs;
  • making a crisis plan before a hard time becomes an emergency;
  • getting links to local help;
  • planning for a school meeting or Individualized Education Program, often called an IEP; or
  • following through on calls, forms, and next steps.

LMHG cannot promise that a resource will be open. These services do not provide emergency help, rides, medicine, inpatient care, or a place to live.

What Support Can Look Like

The first step is to learn what is in the way. A team member may help set a short list of goals. They may link you to a service or plan for a meeting. They can also help you speak up. With your consent, they may work with a therapist, doctor, school, or other group.

Case Management and Community Support are most often paid for by Medicaid. Private pay may be an option for people with other plans. Many plans cover in-home therapy, but we must check. A fee may apply to school meetings if a plan will not pay for them.

How Community-Based Services Fit Together

This page is a guide to three types of help. Case Management helps with plans, basic needs, links to help, and work between providers.

Community Support Services help a client use therapy goals in daily life. A Community Support Associate works with the client and stays in touch with the therapist.

In-Home Therapy is therapy held in the home. The home is the place for the visit. It is not the same as Case Management or Community Support.

Some people need one type of help. Others may need a team with clear roles. LMHG can help you pick a first step.

faqItems.question: Are community-based services the same as therapy?
faqItems.answer: No. They add hands-on help to therapy. In-home therapy is different. It is a therapy visit held at home.

faqItems.question: Are these services only for people with Medicaid?
faqItems.answer: No. Medicaid often pays for Case Management and Community Support. Private pay may be an option for other clients.

faqItems.question: Can LMHG attend an IEP meeting?
faqItems.answer: School support may be available. A fee may apply when insurance does not pay for the meeting.

faqItems.question: Do these services include emergency help?
faqItems.answer: No. A team member may help make a crisis plan, but LMHG does not offer emergency or 24-hour help.

pages.title: Community Support Services in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Community-Based Services  /  Community Support Services in Louisville, KY

Community support services in Louisville, KY, help clients practice therapy goals in daily life. These mental health community support services are led by a Community Support Associate, or CSA. The CSA works with the client and stays in touch with the client's regular therapist. The client still meets with that therapist for therapy.

Call (502) 416-1416

This is a mental health service. It is not crisis care or a general social service. It helps clients use skills outside the therapy room. This is where daily stress and real choices happen.

When This May Help

Community support may help when a client understands a skill in therapy but has a hard time using it day to day. Support is based on the client's own goals and plan. It may include help with:

  • building a steady daily routine.
  • using coping skills during stress.
  • practicing clear communication.
  • speaking up for their needs.
  • breaking a large goal into smaller steps.
  • following through on goals set with a therapist.

Community support is often short-term and focused. The type and amount of help can look different for each client.

What Support Can Look Like

The therapist, client, and CSA work as a team. The therapist guides the therapy plan. The CSA helps the client practice parts of that plan. The work takes place in daily settings when it is a good fit. The team may review what worked. They may also talk about what felt hard and what to try next.

Practice may happen while the client works through a routine, prepares for an appointment, uses a coping skill, or speaks up in a real setting. The activity should connect to a therapy goal. It is not just time spent together in the community.

This team plan gives each provider a clear job. It also keeps therapy and skill practice tied together. Community support does not replace regular therapy.

Community support is mostly Medicaid-based. A private-pay or fee-for-service plan may be possible case by case. LMHG must check cost, fit, and service needs before care starts. The costs and insurance page explains general payment options.

Community Support and Case Management Are Different

Community support focuses on therapy goals in real life. Case Management has a different job. It helps with plans, basic needs, and resources. It can also link providers, schools, insurance plans, or community groups.

Some clients may need one service. Others may need both. LMHG can also explain other community-based mental health services when the right first step is not clear.

faqItems.question: Is community support the same as therapy?
faqItems.answer: No. A CSA helps a client practice therapy goals in daily life. The client still sees a regular therapist.

faqItems.question: Is community support only for Medicaid clients?
faqItems.answer: It is mostly Medicaid-based. A private-pay plan may be possible case by case.

faqItems.question: Where does community support happen?
faqItems.answer: It may happen in a community setting that fits the client's plan. Location and provider availability must be checked first.

faqItems.question: Is community support a crisis service?
faqItems.answer: No. It does not provide emergency or 24-hour help.

pages.title: Conflict Resolution Counseling in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Family Therapy  /  Conflict Resolution Counseling in Louisville, KY

Conflict resolution counseling in Louisville, KY, can help when hard family talks get stuck. They may turn into fights, blame, or long silence. Family conflict resolution counseling does not decide who is right. It slows the pattern so each person can see what is going on. Family relationship conflict counseling can then give hard talks more structure.

Call (502) 416-1416

This work is for conflict among family members who are not a couple. It may include parents and adult children, siblings, parents and grandparents, or members of a blended family. Who takes part will depend on the conflict and the plan.

When This May Help

This work may help when:

  • the same fight keeps coming back.
  • one person gets louder while another shuts down.
  • family members talk through other people. They do not talk to each other.
  • parenting rules cause conflict across homes. They may also cause conflict across generations.
  • siblings or blended family members feel stuck in old roles.
  • hurt feelings make it hard to listen or repair.
  • a hard choice needs a calm and useful talk.

You do not have to wait until each talk becomes a fight. Some families seek help when they first see a pattern. They want to change it before it grows.

What Support Can Look Like

A therapist may help each person see the fight as a whole. This includes what happens before, during, and after it. Sessions can help people slow down and name the real issue. People can learn to listen before they plan a reply. They can also ask for change in a clear way.

People may practice taking a break without dropping the issue. They may learn to return to the talk and set a clear limit. They may also learn to repair harm after someone gets hurt.

The therapist does not pick a winner or take sides. Each person looks at what they do. LMHG cannot promise that things will change.

LMHG may use more than one provider when roles are clear. One person may also have their own therapist. A different therapist may lead this work.

Conflict Resolution, Family Therapy, And Couples Counseling

Family Therapy is broad. It may look at family habits, stress, and daily life. It may also look at roles and how people talk. This page has a tighter aim. It works on a fight that repeats or keeps the family stuck.

Couples Counseling is for two romantic partners. It may cover trust, closeness, sex, money, parenting stress, and big choices. Conflict Resolution Counseling is for non-couple family relationships.

This is therapy. It is not a legal service or custody review. It does not settle a case or make a binding deal. LMHG does not give legal advice or promise a court result.

Architecture Note

Keep /conflict-resolution-counseling/ under Family Therapy for non-couple family relationships. Redirect the old couples-focused route /couples-conflict-resolution/ to /couples-counseling/.

faqItems.question: Is this for couples?
faqItems.answer: No. Couples Counseling is for romantic partners. This service is for conflict among other family members, such as parents and adult children, siblings, parents and grandparents, or members of a blended family.

faqItems.question: Is this the same as mediation?
faqItems.answer: No. This is therapy. It works on habits, how people talk, and how they make up after a fight. LMHG does not settle legal cases or make binding deals.

faqItems.question: Does everyone have to attend every session?
faqItems.answer: Not always. The therapist will help decide who should come. The plan will fit the goals and each provider's role.

faqItems.question: Will the therapist decide who is right?
faqItems.answer: No. The therapist helps people see the pattern and have a more useful talk. The therapist does not take sides.

pages.title: Couples Counseling in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Couples Counseling in Louisville, KY

Couples counseling in Louisville, KY, gives two partners a set place to talk through hard issues with a therapist. LMHG works with same-sex and opposite-sex couples, whether they are married or not. You do not have to agree on the cause of the problem before you start.

Call (502) 416-1416

When This May Help

Couples therapy may help with:

  • Talks that turn into the same argument.
  • A loss of trust.
  • Feeling far apart or alone in the relationship.
  • Parenting stress.
  • Sex or intimacy concerns.
  • Money stress.
  • A major choice or life change.
  • Thoughts about separation.
  • Doubt about whether to stay together.

You can come in when a problem is new or when it has been present for years. Therapy does not promise that the relationship will stay the same or be repaired. It gives both partners a clear way to talk and look at next steps.

What Support Can Look Like

The therapist helps set a plan for the talks. This may include slowing the pace, naming the main issue, and making sure each partner has time to speak. The work can help you hear what the other person means, even when you do not agree.

Both people have a say. They help set the plan. The plan can change as the work moves forward. The pace can slow when talks get tense. The work may start with one hard event. It may also look at a pattern that keeps coming back. You can plan how to talk at home. The topic may be parenting, money, sex, trust, or a major choice.

The therapist does not take one partner's side. The role is to keep the talk fair, clear, and tied to the goals both partners set.

Couples, Relationship, And Marriage Counseling

Couples counseling, relationship counseling, and marriage counseling often mean the same kind of service. At LMHG, Couples Counseling is the main name. It is for two romantic partners who want help with trust, closeness, patterns that repeat, or hard choices about the relationship. Marriage is not required.

Conflict Resolution Counseling has a different focus. It helps with conflict among non-couple family members, such as parents and adult children, siblings, grandparents, or blended family members. Couples Counseling stays focused on the two partners and the goals they bring.

One or both partners may also use Individual Counseling. When that happens, a different therapist should lead the couple's work. Separate therapists keep the roles clear and give each service its own focus.

faqItems.question: Do we have to be married?
faqItems.answer: No. LMHG works with married and unmarried couples, including same-sex and opposite-sex couples.

faqItems.question: Does LMHG offer marriage counseling?
faqItems.answer: Yes. Marriage counseling is available as couples counseling for married partners.

faqItems.question: Is relationship counseling the same as couples counseling?
faqItems.answer: The terms often overlap. LMHG uses Couples Counseling as the main name for work with married or unmarried romantic partners.

faqItems.question: Can one or both partners have individual therapy too?
faqItems.answer: Yes. A separate therapist should provide individual therapy so each clinician has a clear role.

faqItems.question: Will the therapist tell us who is right?
faqItems.answer: No. The therapist does not take sides or give legal or custody opinions.

pages.title: Court-Ordered Services in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Court-Ordered Services in Louisville, KY

Court-ordered services in Louisville, KY, may include some Family Court needs. LMHG reads the order first. It must name a service we offer, or it must let LMHG pick the kind of care. Court-ordered therapy in Louisville, KY, must still fit our scope. Clear words help us check fit before you start.

Call (502) 416-1416

These services may include Co-Parenting, Family Reunification, or mandated therapy tied to CPS involvement. LMHG does not give legal advice. We also cannot promise that a court will accept a service.

When This May Help

This page may help if a Family Court has told you to take part in a service. You may need help with:

  • an order that names a service LMHG provides;
  • Co-Parenting after conflict or a family change;
  • Family Reunification after a break in parent-child contact;
  • therapy ordered due to CPS involvement;
  • proof that you came or took part; or
  • a progress letter, specific recommendations, or testimony.

LMHG does not provide evaluations tied to DUI convictions. This page covers the Family Court services named above.

What Support Can Look Like

Start by sending the court order and any rules about proof. We will review the order. We may ask what the court wants and who must take part.

The order must be clear before the service starts. If its wording does not match a service LMHG provides, the person who was ordered to take part may need to ask the court or an attorney about the next step. LMHG cannot change the order or give legal advice.

The documents we can give depend on the service. They may include attendance confirmation, proof that you took part, a general progress letter, or a treatment recommendation. LMHG may also give testimony or specific recommendations when they fit the service. This is common in some Family Reunification cases, but not in all services.

Before you begin, LMHG will explain what records, updates, fees, or testimony may be part of the service. Those details can differ based on the order and the service being provided.

How These Family Court Services Differ

Co-Parenting Services help one or both parents work on choices for their child and clear talks. Co-Parenting can also start before an order is made.

Family Reunification Services use set steps after a break in contact. Family Reunification is not therapy or counseling. The provider does not take a side. They may give testimony or specific recommendations when the case calls for it.

Court-mandated therapy tied to CPS is therapy. It may focus on your own needs and goals. Individual Counseling may fit. It must match both the order and LMHG's review.

faqItems.question: Does LMHG accept every Family Court order?
faqItems.answer: No. The order must name a service LMHG offers or let LMHG pick the kind of care. We must read the order first.

faqItems.question: Is Family Reunification a type of therapy?
faqItems.answer: No. Family Reunification is its own service. It is not therapy or counseling.

faqItems.question: What documents may LMHG provide?
faqItems.answer: LMHG may provide attendance confirmation, proof that you took part, a general progress letter, a treatment recommendation, testimony, or a specific recommendation. What is available depends on the service and the case.

faqItems.question: Does LMHG provide DUI evaluations?
faqItems.answer: No. LMHG does not provide evaluations tied to DUI convictions.

pages.title: EMDR Therapy in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Trauma Therapy  /  EMDR Therapy in Louisville, KY

EMDR therapy in Louisville, KY, is one trauma-focused option for children, teens, and adults. It can help a person work with a hard memory while staying aware of the present. An EMDR therapist will explain each step and check the pace. You may not need to tell every detail out loud.

Call (502) 416-1416

When This May Help

EMDR may be worth asking about when a past event still shapes daily life. This may include:

  • memories that feel hard to put in the past.
  • strong reactions to reminders or triggers.
  • avoiding people, places, or events.
  • feeling tense, on edge, or quick to react.
  • PTSD symptoms.
  • the effects of childhood trauma, medical trauma, or past relationships.

EMDR is not right for everyone. Age alone does not decide fit. A therapist will look at the person's needs, goals, coping skills, and current level of stress.

What Support Can Look Like

EMDR stands for eye movement desensitization and reprocessing. It is a structured form of therapy. First, the therapist learns what is going on. You then choose what you want to work on. It may be a memory, thought, feeling, or body response.

The therapist may guide your eyes from side to side. They may use taps or sounds instead. The U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs explains that a person holds the memory in mind while also paying attention to a back-and-forth movement or sound. You notice what comes up while the therapist checks your pace and how you feel. Learn more from the VA National Center for PTSD.

EMDR is not always the first step. Some clients need time to build trust. They may need coping skills or a sense of safety first. This early work is often called stabilization. EMDR may also bring up hard feelings, memories, dreams, or body responses. Symptoms can feel worse for a short time. That is why plans and honest check-ins matter.

EMDR for PTSD has support from major health groups. The World Health Organization lists EMDR as one treatment that may be considered for adults with PTSD. This does not mean it is the right fit for every person. Read the WHO PTSD guidance.

EMDR, Trauma Therapy, And Your Main Therapist

Trauma Therapy is broad. It may use talk therapy, grounding skills, CBT, family support, EMDR, or other tools. EMDR is one method within that wider kind of support.

Some clients do EMDR with their main therapist. Others keep a primary therapist through Individual Counseling and also meet with an EMDR-trained therapist. When this team approach fits, each therapist has a clear role. With your permission, they can work toward the same plan.

faqItems.question: Do I have to talk through every detail during EMDR?
faqItems.answer: Often, no. You may need to name the memory or concern, but you may not need to retell the full story. Your therapist will explain what is needed.

faqItems.question: Is EMDR only for PTSD?
faqItems.answer: No. PTSD is one reason a person may ask about EMDR. It may also fit other memories or events that still feel stuck or too intense.

faqItems.question: Can children and teens receive EMDR?
faqItems.answer: Yes, when it is a good fit. The therapist will look at age, needs, coping skills, support, and readiness before making a plan.

faqItems.question: Can EMDR make symptoms worse?
faqItems.answer: Yes, symptoms may get worse for a short time. A therapist can discuss this risk, help you plan, and decide if other support should come first.

pages.title: Family Reunification Services in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Court Ordered Services  /  Family Reunification Services in Louisville, KY

At LMHG, Family Reunification is a paced service. It helps after a break in a parent-child bond. The work is impartial. It is a court-related service. It is not regular therapy or counseling.

Call (502) 416-1416

Either parent may start. A custodial parent cares for the child each day. A reunifying parent has had a break in contact or trust.

When This May Help

Family Reunification Services may fit when a clear court order calls for help with:

  • a long gap in parent-child contact.
  • a custody change.
  • conflict between a parent and child.
  • CPS involvement.
  • family separation or instability.
  • trust and contact after an attachment break.
  • a need for safe, structured pacing.

Goals may include safe contact, clear roles, trust, and better talks. Progress is not promised. The pace must fit the child. It must also fit the case history and court order.

Each case has its own facts. The provider sets a structured pace. The child, case history, safety needs, and court order all matter. Contact may start in small steps. The plan can change as needs shift.

What Support Can Look Like

LMHG first learns the case history. It then reviews the court order. The order must name this service the right way. Both parties must agree to work with LMHG. Both must sign the reunification agreement.

The provider will choose which party to meet first. Case history, contact, and safety needs may guide that choice. The work then moves at a set pace. The provider stays impartial. They do not take sides.

The work may include:

  • documentation of the service.
  • recommendations from the provider.
  • testimony when it is right for the case and role.

Not every case calls for these steps.

How Family Reunification Differs From Therapy

Some people search for reunification therapy in Louisville, KY. At LMHG, this service is not regular therapy or counseling. Family therapy is broader work on family ties, stress, and patterns. It does not replace this court-based process.

Co-Parenting Services may fit shared choices across two homes. The Court-Ordered Services page shows how these services relate.

This service is not a custody evaluation or mediation. It does not include supervised visitation. It is not an emergency crisis service. LMHG does not give legal advice. LMHG cannot take a case without an accurately worded court order. The provider does not promise a court result.

faqItems.question: Is Family Reunification the same as reunification therapy?
faqItems.answer: No. People may use that search term. LMHG treats Family Reunification as its own impartial, court-related service.

faqItems.question: Which parent can contact LMHG?
faqItems.answer: Either parent can reach out. This may be the custodial parent. It may also be the parent who seeks to rebuild contact.

faqItems.question: What is needed before the service can start?
faqItems.answer: LMHG must review an accurately worded court order. Both parties must agree to the service. Both must sign the reunification agreement.

faqItems.question: Will the provider decide custody or take sides?
faqItems.answer: No. The provider is impartial. They do not perform custody evaluations. They may give recommendations or testimony when the case and role call for them.

pages.title: Family Therapy in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Family Therapy in Louisville, KY

Family therapy gives people a place to work on fights and hard talks. Each family can look at what keeps a problem going. Family does not have to mean two parents and kids in one home. It may include siblings, grandparents, grown children, a blended home, or other people who act as family.

Call (502) 416-1416

When This May Help

Family counseling may help when:

  • Talks turn into the same fight
  • A parent and child feel stuck in conflict
  • Siblings have a hard time living or working together
  • Parents and grandparents do not agree on roles
  • Parenting stress affects the whole home
  • A family cannot find a routine that works
  • Family members do not understand why a pattern keeps coming back

The goal is not to blame one person. Therapy can help each person slow down. It can help them hear what others mean. The family can then work on the pattern as a team.

What Support Can Look Like

The first talks focus on what is wrong and what the family wants to change. The therapist can help make a plan for who should join. A parent, child, sibling, grandparent, or other family member may take part.

Not each person has to come to each talk. The right mix must fit the goal and each person's needs. A therapist may meet with the whole group or part of it. The plan can change as needs change. Goals may shift with time. That is okay. The group can make a new plan.

Talks may focus on family roles, home tasks, and why each person acts as they do. Each voice can be heard. Family therapy cannot promise that each tie will be fixed. It gives the family a clear place to do the work.

Family Therapy As A Main Or Added Service

Family therapy can be the main service. It can also be added to other work for a short time. A person in Individual Counseling may ask a parent or sibling to join a few talks. They can work on one concern.

The same can happen in Child Therapy when family talks may help the child. Some needs may fit Parenting Support or Co-Parenting Services. Those types of help have a set focus. Family therapy stays broad and follows the family's goal.

faqItems.question: Who counts as family in family therapy?
faqItems.answer: Family may include parents, children, siblings, grandparents, adult children, blended families, or other people who act as family.

faqItems.question: Does everyone attend every session?
faqItems.answer: No. Who attends is based on the goal, privacy needs, and what the therapist thinks will help.

faqItems.question: Can family therapy be added to individual therapy?
faqItems.answer: Yes. A few family sessions may be added when a family tie is part of the work.

faqItems.question: Does family therapy include mediation or court opinions?
faqItems.answer: No. This service does not provide mediation, custody evaluations, or court opinions.

It can offer a different kind of help than one-on-one or family therapy. For some clients, it can be one part of a larger plan.

Call (502) 416-1416

Group topics and open spots change. LMHG currently has a teen-focused group. Its full description will be added here when it is ready. This does not mean the group has an open spot. Ask about current therapy groups in Louisville, KY, before you plan to join one.

When This May Help

Group therapy may help a client who wants to:

  • learn with people who share a common concern;
  • hear how others handle a hard choice or feeling;
  • practice a skill during a planned session;
  • feel less alone while working on a goal;
  • give and receive help with clear group rules; or
  • add a new kind of learning to other therapy.

The group topic, age range, time, goals, and open spots all matter. A clinician must check that the group fits the client. A group is not the right first step for each person.

What Support Can Look Like

The group leader sets the plan for each session. They explain the rules and help people stay on the topic. A session may have a short lesson, a guided talk, skill practice, or time to think. The exact plan depends on the group.

Clients may learn from one another and help each other. The clinician still leads the work. Groups are not peer-led, anonymous, or drop-in. Group therapy does not give emergency help.

The clinician also explains privacy rules and their limits. Group members are expected to respect each other's privacy, but LMHG cannot promise what another member may share outside the group.

How Group Therapy Differs From Other Therapy

Group therapy includes several clients and one or more clinicians. The shared setting is part of the work. Individual Counseling gives one client more one-on-one time with a therapist. Family Therapy brings family members into therapy to work on shared needs.

Group therapy can add a different kind of practice and shared learning to individual or family therapy. A clinician can help decide whether a group is a useful part of the client's plan.

Group details can change. LMHG will explain the topic, schedule, age range, and any other steps before a client joins.

faqItems.question: Who leads group therapy at LMHG?
faqItems.answer: An LMHG clinician leads each group. The clinician sets the plan, guides the talk, and explains the rules.

faqItems.question: Is a group open right now?
faqItems.answer: Open groups and spots change. Ask LMHG what is open now. Full group details will be posted when they are ready.

faqItems.question: Can group therapy replace individual therapy?
faqItems.answer: Group therapy can be a useful addition to individual or family therapy. Fit depends on the client's needs and the group.

faqItems.question: Is group therapy for crisis help?
faqItems.answer: No. Group therapy is planned care. It does not provide emergency or 24-hour help.

pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Individual Counseling in Louisville, KY

Individual counseling gives you a private place to talk with one main therapist. You can name what is hard. You can set goals. You can shape the work to fit you. Each plan is made for the person. The pace can change as your needs change.

Call (502) 416-1416

When This May Help

Individual therapy may help with concerns that include, but are not limited to:

  • Anxiety or depression
  • Trauma or grief
  • Stress, anger, or feeling stuck
  • Strain in a relationship
  • Work or school stress
  • A move, loss, or other life change

You do not need to know the cause or the right therapy term before you start. You can begin with what is going on now and what you want to change.

What Support Can Look Like

Early talks help you and your therapist learn what brings you in. You can talk about goals, privacy, pace, and what may help. You can then pick ways to work on the need.

Because this is one-on-one work, the plan can bend. You can spend more time on one need. You can change a goal. You can slow down when a topic needs more care.

Most clients have one main therapist they see on a regular basis. At times, a second therapist may help with a focused need. For example, you may keep your main therapist and also meet with an EMDR therapist. Each therapist has a clear role.

The same rule can help keep boundaries clear when couples work is added. One therapist may meet with the couple, while a different therapist provides one-on-one support.

LMHG accepts Medicaid, commercial insurance, and private pay when they fit the service and plan. See Costs and Insurance for more details.

Individual Counseling And Related Therapy

Individual counseling is the broad one-on-one service. Adult Counseling speaks to adult needs, while Teen Therapy is written for teens and their families. Your therapist can help decide whether a more focused page or service fits your needs.

If family or couple talks may help, that work can be added with clear roles. A different therapist may lead that service so each therapist's role stays clear.

faqItems.question: Is individual counseling private?
faqItems.answer: Yes. It is private one-on-one therapy, with legal and safety limits. Your therapist will explain those limits.

faqItems.question: How soon can I start?
faqItems.answer: Clients can often be seen within 7 to 14 days. A wait may be longer if you need a therapist with a specific trait or skill.

faqItems.question: Can I work with more than one therapist?
faqItems.answer: Yes, when it helps. You may have one main therapist and another for a focused need. Their roles stay clear.

faqItems.question: Do I need to choose a therapy method first?
faqItems.answer: No. You and your therapist can choose methods based on your needs and why you reached out.

pages.title: Parenting Support in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Child Therapy  /  Parenting Support in Louisville, KY

Parenting support in Louisville, KY, helps parents work through hard moments at home. Parent counseling gives them a place to slow down, spot patterns, and choose clear next steps. Caregiver guidance may focus on behavior, big feelings, limits, routines, school stress, or family strain.

Call (502) 416-1416

This service is for parents. It is part of child therapy and gives parents focused help with what is happening at home. It is not a parenting class, a program that gives a certificate, a custody review, or a legal service.

When This May Help

Parenting support may fit when:

  • Daily routines turn into fights or shutdowns.
  • A child has big reactions that are hard to calm.
  • Parents do not agree on rules or limits.
  • School stress is making home life harder.
  • Bedtime, mornings, screens, or changes feel stuck.
  • A loss, move, divorce, or other stress has changed home life.
  • A parent feels worn down and is not sure what to try.

You do not need to know the root cause before reaching out. Start with what you can see and what feels hard now.

What Support Can Look Like

Parenting support is parent-only work. It may include parent sessions, check-ins, and plans for home routines. A therapist may help parents notice what happens before conflict. Parents can practice clear limits, calm replies, and steady follow-through.

The work may also cover how to repair after an argument. School or family contact may be part of the plan when it is useful and allowed.

LMHG does not assume one plan fits every home. Some parents need a few visits for one concern. Some children need their own therapist or family sessions through another part of child therapy. More than one provider may help when each person has a clear need.

How Parenting Support Is Different

Parenting support is not a class. It does not follow one set lesson plan or promise a certificate. It is parent-only counseling and guidance based on what is happening in your home.

Family therapy looks at the wider family pattern. It may include parents, children, siblings, grandparents, or others. Parenting support keeps the main focus on the adult who cares for the child.

Child therapy is the parent service that includes parenting support. A child may have their own therapy while a parent also gets help with rules, routines, and home stress.

For separated parents who need help working with each other, co-parenting services may be a better fit.

faqItems.question: Is parenting support a class?
faqItems.answer: No. It is counseling and guidance for parents. It does not give a class certificate.

faqItems.question: Does the child attend parenting support sessions?
faqItems.answer: No. Parenting support is for parents. A child may take part in another part of child therapy or family therapy when that fits the plan.

faqItems.question: Can this connect with child therapy?
faqItems.answer: Yes. A child may have their own therapist while a parent gets help with home life.

faqItems.question: Does this include custody advice?
faqItems.answer: No. Parenting support does not include legal advice, custody opinions, custody reviews, or court outcome promises.

pages.title: Play Therapy in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Child Therapy  /  Play Therapy in Louisville, KY

Play therapy is typically used with children ages 2 to 12. This therapy through play helps a child show feelings that may be hard to explain with words. A therapist may use toys, art, stories, and games with a clear goal. It is not just free play, a reward, or a way to keep a child busy.

Call (502) 416-1416

The age range is not a hard rule. Some play-based techniques may also be used with teens or adults when they fit the person and the therapy goal.

At LMHG, play therapy may fit a child who needs help with feelings, behavior, stress, grief, trauma, school, or family change. Parents take part in a way that fits the child. Younger children often need more parent help.

When This May Help

Play therapy may help when a child has:

  • Big feelings that come out fast.
  • Meltdowns, shutdowns, or strong fear.
  • Trouble with sleep, school, or daily routines.
  • More anger, worry, or sadness.
  • Stress after loss, a move, divorce, or family conflict.
  • Hard behavior at home or school.
  • Trouble talking about something that happened.

Parents often know something feels wrong before they know why. Starting with what you see is enough.

What Support Can Look Like

The therapist pays close attention to the child's play. They may notice themes, choices, stress, and how the child responds to limits. Play can help the child name feelings, build trust, practice skills, or work through hard events at the child's pace.

Parent support will fit the child and family. It may include parent check-ins, help with home routines, family sessions, school contact, or ideas to try between visits. Younger children will often need more parent help than older children.

Some families need more than one type of support. The team can help decide which service is the best place to start.

How Play Therapy Is Different From Other Child Services

Play therapy is one type of child therapy. Child therapy is the broad service. It may include talk, play, parent help, family work, school help, or coping skills.

Child behavioral therapy has a closer focus on behavior patterns, triggers, routines, and daily skills. It may fit when the main concern is repeated behavior at home or school.

Play therapy may fit when a child needs a way to show feelings through play. These paths can overlap. The right start depends on the child's age, what is happening, and what the family needs.

Family therapy may help when the main concern is a pattern between family members.

faqItems.question: What ages is play therapy for?
faqItems.answer: It is typically used with children ages 2 to 12. This is not a hard rule. Some play-based techniques may also fit teens or adults.

faqItems.question: How involved are parents?
faqItems.answer: Parent involvement is based on the child. Younger children often need more parent help.

faqItems.question: How is play therapy different from ordinary play?
faqItems.answer: Play therapy has a therapy goal. The therapist uses play to learn about needs and help the child practice skills.

faqItems.question: How do we get started?
faqItems.answer: Use the Reach Out intake form. LMHG can help sort out service fit and provider fit.

pages.title: Counseling Services in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services

Counseling services in Louisville, KY, can help with stress, family change, trauma, and daily needs. LMHG offers therapy, counseling, and hands-on help. You do not need to know the right name for the help you need.

Call (502) 416-1416

Our mental health services in Louisville, KY, are for kids, teens, adults, couples, and families. Some people need one therapist. Others may need a small team. Each team member has a clear role. The first talk helps us learn what may fit.

Therapy And Counseling Services

Individual Counseling gives one person a private place to talk. It may help with stress, grief, trauma, or a life change. Child Therapy helps parents learn what a child may need. Family Therapy helps with fights and hard talks at home.

Couples Counseling is for two partners. It may help with trust, closeness, or hard talks. Group Therapy brings clients and a group leader together. The group can learn and use new skills. Topics and open spots may change.

Focused And Court-Related Services

Trauma Therapy may help when a past event still affects life now. The plan may use talk, grounding skills, EMDR, or other tools.

Court-Ordered Services may cover some Family Court needs. LMHG reads the order first. We do not give legal advice. We cannot say what a court will accept.

Support Beyond The Therapy Room

Community-Based Mental Health Services can help when daily needs get in the way. This group of services includes Case Management and Community Support. It may also include work with a school or local group.

Case Management helps with plans, links to aid, and team work. Community Support helps clients use therapy goals in daily life. In-home therapy is not the same. It is a therapy visit held at home when that place is a good fit.

Which Service May Fit?

Start with the need that feels most clear. A parent may start with Child Therapy. A person with a hard memory may start with Trauma Therapy. A person who needs help with food, a home, school, or a large team may start with Community-Based Services.

It is fine to be unsure. A short request can share how to reach you and the kind of help you want. LMHG can then talk with you about fit, wait time, cost, and who may help.

faqItems.question: Do I need to choose a service before I reach out?
faqItems.answer: No. You can say what is happening and who needs help. LMHG can talk with you about which service may fit.

faqItems.question: Are all services therapy?
faqItems.answer: No. LMHG offers therapy and counseling, but it also offers Case Management and Community Support. Each service has a different role.

faqItems.question: Can one person have more than one provider?
faqItems.answer: Yes, when it helps and each provider has a clear role. A main therapist may work with another therapist or support team member.

faqItems.question: How do I ask about cost?
faqItems.answer: Share your insurance plan or ask about private pay. LMHG will check the service, plan, and coverage before work starts.

Home  /  Specialties

Specialized therapy in Louisville, KY, may help when you know what you want to work on. LMHG has focused help for trauma, family needs, child behavior, parents, and daily life. Each type of help links back to a broad service. You can start broad if that feels more clear.

Call (502) 416-1416

Specialty counseling in Louisville, KY, does not mean you must pick a method on your own. These pages tell you what each option is. They also say who it may help and what it cannot do. A provider can help you think about fit.

Support By Age Or Concern

Teen Therapy is for teens and families. It may help with mood, worry, school stress, trauma, or fights at home. Adult Counseling gives adults a place to work on stress, grief, a close tie, or a big life change.

Anxiety and Depression Therapy focuses on worry, fear, low mood, and daily strain. LMHG offers therapy and counseling. It does not manage medicine.

Child And Parent Specialties

Child Behavioral Therapy helps parents look at what a child does at home or school. Play Therapy uses play with a clear goal. It is most often used with kids ages 2 to 12.

Parent-Child Attachment Therapy works on trust between a child and parent. Parenting Support helps with limits, home tasks, big feelings, and family stress.

Trauma And Focused Therapy

EMDR Therapy is one set way to work on trauma. It may fit when a past event still feels stuck or too strong. EMDR is not right for each person. Trust and coping skills may need to come first.

The broad Trauma Therapy page may be a better start if a past event still affects you but you do not know which method may fit.

Family And Court-Related Specialties

Co-Parenting Services help parents make child-focused choices. The work may start with a parent or a court order. It is not mediation or a custody review.

Family Reunification Services use a slow, fair process after a break in parent-child contact or trust. Conflict Resolution Counseling is for repeat fights among family members who are not a couple.

Practical Mental Health Support

Case Management helps with plans, basic needs, links to aid, and team work. Community Support Services help clients use therapy goals in daily life with a support worker.

These services are not therapy. They may work next to therapy when a client needs more hands-on help.

pages.title: Trauma Therapy in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Trauma Therapy in Louisville, KY

Trauma therapy in Louisville, KY, may help adults, teens, and families. It may fit when a past event still affects life now. A trauma-informed therapist will work at a pace that fits the person. We do not push clients to tell all the facts at once.

Call (502) 416-1416

When This May Help

People can react to hard events in many ways. Trauma therapy may help you or a family member with:

  • hard memories that keep coming back.
  • avoiding people, places, or events.
  • feeling tense, on edge, or quick to react.
  • the effects of childhood trauma or medical trauma.
  • trauma from past relationships.
  • stress that affects home, school, work, or family life.

You do not need to know the right term for what is going on. You can start with what you notice now and what you want help with.

What Support Can Look Like

Trauma therapy does not always start with the hardest part of the story. Early work may focus on coping skills, safety, trust, and ways to feel more steady. Deeper work can come later if it fits your needs.

The plan may use talk therapy, grounding skills, methods that work on thoughts and actions, family support, or another approach. Adults and teens may work one-on-one through Individual Counseling. A child may use Child Therapy with help from a parent or caregiver. A family may also use Family Therapy when trauma has changed trust, roles, or daily life.

Your therapist will help choose the pace and tools. The plan can change as needs change. No one method is right for every person.

How Trauma Therapy And EMDR Are Different

Trauma therapy is a broad kind of support. It can include many ways to build skills and work through past events. EMDR Therapy is one trauma-focused option within that larger plan.

EMDR may fit some clients. It is not the only path. Some people start with coping skills or talk therapy. Others may use EMDR later. The choice depends on age, goals, stress, and how ready the person feels for deeper work.

faqItems.question: Do I have to tell the whole story right away?
faqItems.answer: No. You can start with what is affecting you now. Therapy can first focus on trust, coping skills, safety, and feeling more steady.

faqItems.question: Is EMDR always part of trauma therapy?
faqItems.answer: No. EMDR is one option. Trauma therapy may also use talk therapy, grounding skills, methods that work on thoughts and actions, child therapy, or family support.

faqItems.question: Can children and teens receive trauma therapy?
faqItems.answer: Yes, when it fits their needs. The plan may include a parent or caregiver, family sessions, or child-focused tools.

faqItems.question: Does LMHG provide crisis care or medication management?
faqItems.answer: No. LMHG does not provide emergency crisis care, inpatient or residential care, or medication management. LMHG also does not provide trauma evaluations for court cases or custody opinions.
